index.html.title: MGM Character Controller | MGM Character Controller
index.html.summary: MGM Character Controller MGM Character Controller is a modular, high-performance character locomotion package for Unity 6, built entirely on the DOTS stack (Entities, Unity.Physics, Burst, Jobs). It provides a state-machine-driven approach to character movement with full source generation, making it both easy to extend and blazingly fast at runtime. Key Features Pure ECS locomotion with Burst-compiled jobs for maximum throughput State-machine-driven movement with a clear lifecycle per state Source-generated boilerplate via Roslyn analyzers, so you focus on gameplay logic Collide-and-slide physics with automatic step detection, slope handling, and ground snapping Moving platform support with velocity inheritance and rotation handling Hybrid GameObject layer for seamless integration with animations, audio, and cameras Built-in debug visualization for contact points, movement paths, and collision impulses Editor scaffolding tools that generate the entire state machine folder structure in one click Quick Links Getting Started - Installation and your first character Architecture Overview - How the pieces fit together The Execution Loop - What happens each fixed update Creating a State Machine - Step-by-step guide Writing Custom States - The state lifecycle in depth API Reference - Full API documentation Requirements Requirement Version Unity 6000.3+ Entities 1.4.2 Unity.Physics 1.4.2 Burst (bundled with Entities) Jobs (bundled with Entities) Package Info Package name com.wayngamesassets.mgm.charactercontroller Version 0.3.0 Author WAYN Games License Proprietary

manual/architecture.html.title: Architecture Overview | MGM Character Controller
manual/architecture.html.summary:
Architecture Overview This page explains the high-level design of the MGM Character Controller: the entity types, the role of each layer, and how data flows through the system. Design Philosophy The package is built around three principles: Performance first - All locomotion logic runs in Burst-compiled parallel jobs. No managed allocations on the hot path. Separation of concerns - Physics simulation (ECS) is decoupled from presentation (GameObjects). You can run thousands of characters without any GameObjects at all. Extensibility through composition - Instead of a monolithic character controller, you compose behavior from a state machine, a physics profile, an input component, and a context, all of which are user-defined types. Entity Types The package defines three distinct entity roles. Understanding them is essential. Locomotor (Pure ECS) The Locomotor is the primary character entity. It lives entirely in ECS and carries all the components needed for physics-based movement: Component Purpose LocalTransform World position and rotation PhysicsCollider Capsule collider for collision detection PhysicsVelocity Required by Unity.Physics (mostly zeroed; the package drives movement directly) LocomotionVelocity The character's actual velocity, maintained by the state machine LocomotionGravity Gravity vector applied to this character LocomotionContact Current surface contact information (normal, type, entity) CharacterPhysicsProfileBlob Baked physics configuration (capsule size, mass, slopes) LocomotionProfileBlob Baked gameplay profile (speeds, jump force, etc.) TStateMachine Source-generated state machine component (current state + dispatch) TInput User-defined input component TStateData User-defined per-state persistent data The Locomotor is a kinematic body. Unlike Unity.Physics dynamic bodies, it does not respond to forces automatically. Instead, the locomotion job computes displacement each frame and moves the collider through the world using a collide-and-slide algorithm. Model (Hybrid ECS + GameObject) The Model is an optional entity that provides visual representation. It carries a reference to a GameObject prefab that gets instantiated at runtime. The model entity follows the Locomotor entity via the CharacterFollower component. Why separate them? Because: Locomotion logic runs in parallel Burst jobs that cannot touch GameObjects Visual updates (animation, particles, audio) need MonoBehaviour access You can run headless simulations (server, AI training) with zero GameObjects Player (Input Source) The Player entity represents an input source. It owns a ControlledCharacters buffer that lists which Locomotor entities it controls. A Player also has: A PlayerGameObjectPrefab referencing a GameObject with camera, input system, and UI A PlayerGameObjectInstance linking to the instantiated runtime copy An LocomotionInputAsset that reads hardware input and maps it to the input component Players are managed by the PlayerGOManagerSystem, which instantiates and destroys their GameObjects as players join and leave. Assembly Structure The package is split into three runtime assemblies: Wayn.Games.Locomotion.Runtime Core ECS logic (state machine, physics, components, systems) Wayn.Games.Locomotion.Hybrid GameObject bridge (transform sync, entity-GO mapping) Wayn.Games.Locomotion.Editor Editor tools (scaffolding, inspectors) Your game code references Runtime and optionally Hybrid. The Editor assembly is editor-only and provides scaffolding menus. Data Flow Here is how data flows through the system each fixed update: flowchart TD HW["Hardware Input"] PIA["PlayerInputAsset.ReadInputsFromAsset()
MonoBehaviour, Update rate"] ICI["ICharacterLocomotionInput.ApplyInputsToCharacter()
Accumulates triggers"] IRS["InputReaderSystem (generated)
Fixed update, writes TInput component"] LJ["LocomotionJob (Burst, parallel)
Fixed update, reads all components"] TR["Transform updated on Locomotor entity"] CFS["CharacterFollowerSystem
Syncs Model entity to Locomotor"] EGO["EntityGoTransformSyncSystem (Hybrid)
Copies entity transforms to GameObjects"] GO["GameObject renders on screen"] HW --> PIA --> ICI --> IRS --> LJ subgraph LocomotionJob S1["SolveOverlaps"] S2["HandleExternalImpulse"] S3["OnIntegrateState"] S4["OnResolveStateCollisions (loop)"] S5["SnapToGround"] S6["ComputeInheritedDisplacement"] S7["EvaluateTransitions"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 end LJ --> LocomotionJob --> TR --> CFS --> EGO --> GO Source Generation A Roslyn source generator reads your state enum annotated with [LocomotionStateMachine] and [LocomotionState] attributes and produces: State machine component - An IComponentData struct that holds the current state and dispatches to the correct state implementation Locomotion system - An ISystem that schedules the LocomotionJob each fixed update Input reader system - An ISystem that reads input from the PlayerInputAsset and writes it to the TInput component Player input manager - Manages per-player input asset instances This means you never write scheduling code, component type handle boilerplate, or state dispatch switch statements. You define your states, annotate your enum, and the generator handles the rest. Blob Assets Configuration data (physics profiles, locomotion profiles, debug config) is stored as blob assets rather than regular components. Blob assets are: Immutable at runtime (safe for parallel access) Shared across entities with identical configuration (memory efficient) Created during baking from ScriptableObject authoring assets This means changing a profile value requires re-baking (exiting and re-entering play mode, or modifying the asset in the editor). Next Steps The Execution Loop - Detailed breakdown of each step in the locomotion job Creating a State Machine - How to define your own state machine

manual/creating-state-machine.html.title: Creating a State Machine | MGM Character Controller
manual/creating-state-machine.html.summary: Creating a State Machine This guide explains how to create a complete locomotion state machine from scratch, either using the editor scaffolding tool or by hand. Using the Editor Tool (Recommended) The fastest way to create a state machine is through the Unity editor menu: Right-click in the Project window where you want the state machine folder Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > Locomotion > StateMachine Type a name for your state machine (e.g., Vehicle, Swimming, Flying) Press Enter The tool generates all required files with proper naming, namespaces, and wiring. You can immediately start customizing the generated code. Understanding What Gets Generated When you create a state machine named MyCharacter, the tool generates these types: The State Enum [LocomotionStateMachine("MyCharacterStateMachine", typeof(MyCharacterContext), typeof(MyCharacterProfile), typeof(MyCharacterInput), typeof(MyCharacterStateData))] public enum MyCharacterStates : byte { [LocomotionState(typeof(MyCharacterState))] MyCharacter } This enum is the heart of the state machine. The [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute tells the source generator: Name: What to call the generated state machine component and systems Context: The struct that carries all data through the state lifecycle Profile: The struct holding gameplay configuration (speeds, forces) Input: The struct receiving player or AI commands StateData: The struct holding per-state persistent data (jump counts, timers) Each enum value has a [LocomotionState] attribute linking it to a concrete state struct. The Context public struct MyCharacterContext : ILocomotionContext<MyCharacterProfile, MyCharacterInput, MyCharacterStateData> { public BaseLocomotionContext BaseContext { get; set; } public CharacterPhysicsProfile CharacterPhysicsProfile { get; set; } public MyCharacterProfile Profile { get; set; } public MyCharacterInput Input { get; set; } public MyCharacterStateData StateData { get; set; } } The context is a data bag that is passed by reference through every state lifecycle method. It wraps: BaseLocomotionContext - The core physics state (position, velocity, displacement, contacts, gravity) CharacterPhysicsProfile - The baked capsule/physics configuration Your custom profile, input, and state data Tip If you frequently modify BaseContext fields, declare it as a public field rather than using the auto-property. This lets you use ref access: ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; The Profile [Serializable] public struct MyCharacterProfile : ILocomotionProfile { // Add your configuration fields here: // public float MaxSpeed; // public float JumpForce; } Profile data is baked into a blob asset during entity conversion. It is immutable at runtime and shared across all entities that reference the same profile asset. Put all tunable gameplay parameters here. The Input public struct MyCharacterInput : ICharacterLocomotionInput<MyCharacterInput> { // Add your input fields here: // public float3 Movement; // public float JumpTrigger; public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in MyCharacterInput playerInputs) { // Copy or accumulate input values here } } The input struct is an ECS component. It receives values from the PlayerInputAsset at the Update rate and accumulates them for the fixed update. The ApplyInputsToCharacter method is called every Update frame by the generated input reader system. Warning Trigger inputs (like jump) must be accumulated, not overwritten. If the Update rate is higher than the fixed update rate, a trigger press could be missed. Accumulate with JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger and consume it in your state logic by resetting it to zero. The StateData public struct MyCharacterStateData : IComponentData { // Add per-state persistent data here: // public int JumpCount; // public float StateTimer; } State data persists across frames and state transitions. Unlike the context (which is rebuilt each frame from components), state data is stored as an ECS component and survives between frames. The Profile Asset [CreateAssetMenu(...)] public class MyCharacterProfileAsset : LocomotionProfileAsset<MyCharacterProfile> { // Serialized fields matching your profile struct public override ref MyCharacterProfile BuildBlobAsset(ref BlobBuilder blobBuilder) { ref MyCharacterProfile profile = ref blobBuilder.ConstructRoot<MyCharacterProfile>(); // Copy serialized fields to blob return ref profile; } } This ScriptableObject is what you assign in the Inspector. It serializes your profile data and builds the blob asset during baking. The Input Asset [CreateAssetMenu(...)] public class MyCharacterPlayerInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset<MyCharacterInput> { public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out MyCharacterInput inputs) { // Read from Input System and populate inputs } public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance instance) { // Cache camera, PlayerInput component, etc. } } The input asset reads hardware input and converts it to your input struct. It is a ScriptableObject that gets instantiated per player at runtime, so each player can cache their own camera and input device references. The Authoring Components Two authoring MonoBehaviours are generated: Character Locomotor Authoring - Placed on the character entity in the sub-scene. References the profile asset and configures the physics profile. Player Character Authoring - Placed on the player entity. References the character to control and the input asset. The Locomotion System public partial struct MyCharacterLocomotionSystem : ILocomotionSystem< MyCharacterStates, MyCharacterContext, MyCharacterProfile, MyCharacterInput, MyCharacterStateData, MyCharacterStateMachine>, ISystem { // Implement OnCreateLocomotionSystem, OnUpdateLocomotionSystem, // and OnDestroyLocomotionSystem to hook into system lifecycle } Your system implements the ILocomotionSystem interface, which provides the bridge between your types and the generated LocomotionJob. The source generator supplies the scheduling and dispatch logic. What the Source Generator Produces In addition to the files you write, the Roslyn source generator produces (at compile time, invisible in the Project window): State machine component (MyCharacterStateMachine) — An IComponentData struct with a CurrentState field and dispatch methods that call the correct state implementation based on the enum value Input reader system — Reads from the PlayerInputAsset each Update frame and writes to the TInput component ISystem lifecycle methods — Generated OnCreate, OnUpdate, and OnDestroy on your locomotion system struct that schedule the LocomotionJob and manage type handles You never need to write or maintain this code. The generator reads your enum and produces it automatically. Adding a New State After the initial generation, use the editor menu to add states: Right-click in the Project window Select the generated menu item for your state machine (e.g., Assets > Create > WAYN Games > Locomotion > MyCharacter > State) Name the state (e.g., Swimming) This generates a new state struct file and automatically adds it to your state enum with the [LocomotionState] attribute. Alternatively, create the state struct manually and add the enum entry yourself. See Writing Custom States for the struct template. Manual Creation If you prefer to create everything by hand (or need to understand what the tools do), follow this checklist: Create a States enum with [LocomotionStateMachine] attribute Create a context struct implementing ILocomotionContext<TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Create a profile struct implementing ILocomotionProfile Create an input struct implementing ICharacterLocomotionInput<TInput> Create a state data struct implementing IComponentData Create at least one state struct implementing ILocomotionState<TStates, TContext, TProfile, TInput, TStateData> Add [LocomotionState(typeof(YourState))] to each enum value Create a LocomotionProfileAsset<TProfile> ScriptableObject Create a PlayerInputAsset<TInput> ScriptableObject Create a BaseCharacterLocomotorAuthoring<TProfile> subclass with its Baker Create a BasePlayerCharacterAuthoring subclass Create a system implementing ILocomotionSystem<...> and ISystem Register the generic input component: [assembly: RegisterGenericComponentType(typeof(LocomotionInputAsset<TInput>))] The editor tool does all of this in one click, which is why it is recommended. Next Steps Writing Custom States - Implement state logic The Execution Loop - Understand when each method is called

manual/debug-visualization.html.title: Debug Visualization | MGM Character Controller
manual/debug-visualization.html.summary: Debug Visualization The MGM Character Controller includes a built-in debug visualization system that renders contact points, movement paths, and collision impulses directly in the Scene view. This is invaluable for diagnosing movement issues, tuning physics parameters, and understanding what the locomotion system is doing each frame. Enabling Debug Visualization Debug visualization is opt-in per entity: Select the character entity's authoring GameObject in the sub-scene Add a LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoring component Configure which visualizations you want to see Enter Play mode The debug config is baked into a blob asset, so changes require re-entering Play mode. Debug Data Types Path Points Path points show the character's position at each stage of the execution loop. They visualize the trajectory the character takes through a single frame, including each bounce of the collide-and-slide algorithm. Contact Points Contact points mark where the character's collider touches the environment. They are color-coded by contact type: Contact Type What It Means Ground The surface is walkable (slope below MaxSlope) Wall The surface is too steep to walk on Ceiling The surface is above the character None No contact this frame Impulse Points Impulse points show where collision forces are applied, both forces the character receives and forces it applies to other bodies. These help diagnose unexpected velocity changes or interactions with dynamic objects. Debug Stages Each visualization point is tagged with the execution stage where it was recorded: Stage Description Idle Before any processing Integrate During OnIntegrateState Collide During OnResolveStateCollisions SnapToGround During the ground snapping step ComputeInheritedDisplacement During platform velocity computation EvaluateTransition During state transition evaluation This lets you pinpoint exactly which stage is producing unexpected behavior. Components and Systems LocomotionDebugConfig A blob asset storing debug configuration per entity. Controls which stages and data types are visualized. LocomotionDebugRecorder A static utility that records debug points into dynamic buffers during job execution. Called from within the locomotion job when debug buffers are present. Debug Buffers Three IBufferElementData types store the recorded points: LocomotionDebugPathPoint - Position + stage + frame index LocomotionDebugContactPoint - Position + normal + contact type + stage + frame index LocomotionDebugImpulsePoint - Position + impulse vector + body type + frame index LocomotionDebugDisplaySystem Reads the debug buffers and renders gizmos in the Scene view using Debug.DrawLine and Debug.DrawRay. LocomotionDebugFrameCounterSystem Maintains a global frame counter used to tag debug points. This allows the display system to distinguish between points from different frames and optionally show history. Performance Considerations Debug visualization adds overhead: Memory: Each entity with debug enabled allocates three dynamic buffers that grow each frame CPU: Recording points in the locomotion job adds branch checks and buffer writes Rendering: Drawing gizmos happens on the main thread For this reason: Only enable debug on entities you are actively investigating Disable debug visualization in builds The debug components are automatically excluded when the authoring component is not present Custom Debug Inspector The LocomotionDebugConfigAuthoringEditor provides a custom Inspector for the debug config authoring component. It shows a visual preview of the configured debug options and provides toggles for each data type and stage. Tips for Debugging Character falls through ground Enable path points and ground snap visualization. Check if: The snap-to-ground raycast is reaching the ground (path point should show a downward segment) The contact type at the end of the frame is Ground Character gets stuck on walls Enable contact points. Check if: The contact is classified as Wall vs Ground The MaxSlope threshold is correct Step detection is attempting and failing (check MaxStepHeight) Character launched unexpectedly Enable impulse points. Check if: External impulses are being applied (from CharacterContactSystem) A dynamic body collision is injecting unexpected velocity Platform inheritance is double-applied State transitions not firing This is a logic issue, not a physics issue. Add Debug.Log calls in your transition helper methods (temporarily, for non-Burst debugging) or check the state machine component in the Entity Inspector. Profiler Integration Beyond visual debugging, the package integrates with Unity's Profiler: ProfilerMarkerExtensions Burst-compiled profiler marker utilities that add custom markers to the Profiler timeline. These markers show up under the locomotion job's execution, helping you identify which stage is consuming the most time. LocomotionProfilerHUD An optional MonoBehaviour (in the Hybrid assembly) that renders a simple in-game profiler overlay showing FPS and locomotion system timing. Next Steps The Execution Loop - Understand what each debug stage corresponds to Physics Configuration - Tuning parameters informed by debug data

manual/execution-loop.html.title: The Execution Loop | MGM Character Controller
manual/execution-loop.html.summary:
The Execution Loop Every fixed update, the locomotion job processes each character entity through a precise sequence of steps. Understanding this sequence is critical for writing correct state logic. Overview The locomotion job runs as a Burst-compiled IJobChunk scheduled in the FixedStepSimulationSystemGroup, after the physics step. Each character is processed independently and in parallel. The steps execute in this exact order: Step Phase Your Code 1 SolveOverlaps 2 HandleExternalImpulse 3 Integrate OnIntegrateState 4 Collide-and-Slide (loop) OnResolveStateCollisions 5 SnapToGround 6 ComputeInheritedDisplacement 7 EvaluateTransitions EvaluateTransitions There is no OnUpdateState method. The integration and collision resolution phases together form the "update" of your state. Transitions are evaluated once, at the very end, after all physics is resolved. Step-by-Step Breakdown 1. SolveOverlaps Before any movement happens, the system checks whether the character's collider is currently overlapping with any other collider. This can happen when: A moving platform pushes the character into a wall Another character occupies the same space The character was teleported into geometry The overlap solver attempts to push the character out of the overlap. If it cannot (e.g., the character is pinched between two moving surfaces), it tracks the overlap state: OverlapState Meaning None No overlap detected Push Character was pushed out successfully Pinch Character is squeezed but not fully trapped Crush Character is fully embedded with no escape You can read ctx.BaseContext.OverlapState in your state logic to react to crushing situations (e.g., kill the character). 2. HandleExternalImpulse The CharacterContactSystem runs before the locomotion job and collects velocity impulses from external sources, such as: Dynamic rigid bodies that have collided with the character Other characters pushing against this one Explosions or force fields These impulses are applied to the character's velocity before your state logic runs, so your OnIntegrateState sees the updated velocity. This is why you should not clear velocity unconditionally at the start of integration unless you intend to discard external forces. 3. Integrate (OnIntegrateState) This is where your state logic computes velocities and displacements for the current frame. The system calls your state's OnIntegrateState method. In this step, you should: Compute acceleration from input (movement direction, jump force) Apply gravity acceleration Call baseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements() to convert velocities into displacements What you should NOT do here: Move the character's position directly (that happens in the collision step) Add platform velocity (the ComputeInheritedDisplacement step handles that automatically) Read contact information from this frame (contacts haven't been resolved yet; you see last frame's contacts) The Kinematic Equation UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements applies the standard kinematic equations: displacement += velocity * dt + 0.5 * acceleration * dt^2 velocity += acceleration * dt This is done separately for horizontal and vertical components, allowing different behavior for each (e.g., gravity only affects vertical, movement input only affects horizontal). 4. Collide-and-Slide (OnResolveStateCollisions) After integration computes the desired displacement, the character needs to actually move through the world while respecting colliders. This is the collide-and-slide loop. The system calls your state's OnResolveStateCollisions, which should call baseContext.Move(). The Move() method: Projects the displacement along the current contact surface (if any) Casts the collider forward by the projected displacement If a collision is detected, updates the contact information and reduces the remaining displacement Returns control to the system The system re-invokes OnResolveStateCollisions as long as there is remaining displacement, up to CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxBounce iterations. This multi-bounce approach allows the character to slide along walls, step over obstacles, and navigate complex geometry. Contact-Dependent Behavior in Move() The Move() method applies different displacement corrections depending on the contact type: Contact Type Behavior None Displacement is applied as-is (free movement in air) Ground Vertical displacement is cancelled; horizontal displacement follows the ground slope Wall Displacement is projected along the wall surface; step detection is attempted Ceiling Vertical displacement is projected along the ceiling; horizontal displacement is constrained Step Detection When a wall contact is detected, the system checks whether it could be a step: The slope must exceed MaxSlope The character must be moving into the slope The contact height must be below MaxStepHeight If all conditions are met, PhysicsOperations.TryStep attempts to move the character up and over the step. 5. SnapToGround After all collision resolution is complete, the system performs a downward raycast to detect the ground surface below the character. This ensures the character stays grounded when walking down slopes or stairs, rather than becoming airborne for a frame. The snap distance is computed by your context's ComputeGroundSnappingDistance method. The default implementation accounts for: The maximum step height The current slope angle The character's horizontal speed (faster characters need to check further down) Platform vertical velocity (if riding a descending platform) If a ground surface is found within the snap distance, the character's position is adjusted downward and the contact information is updated. 6. ComputeInheritedDisplacement If the character is standing on a moving platform (a kinematic body with the Platform tag), this step computes the velocity and rotation the character inherits from the platform. The inherited values are stored in: baseContext.PlatformHorizontalVelocity baseContext.PlatformVerticalVelocity baseContext.InheritedRotation These are available to your state logic in the next frame's OnIntegrateState. The actual position adjustment from platform movement is applied automatically, which is why you should never add platform velocity manually in your state logic. 7. EvaluateTransitions The final step calls your state's EvaluateTransitions method once. By this point: The character has moved to its final position All contacts are resolved and up-to-date Platform inheritance is computed Your transition logic can inspect the full post-physics state to decide whether to change states. The convention is to chain transition checks with early returns: internal static void EvaluateTransitions(ref MyContext ctx, out MyStates newState) { if (IdleToJumping(ref ctx, out newState)) return; if (IdleToMoving(ref ctx, out newState)) return; if (IdleToFalling(ref ctx, out newState)) return; } The order of checks determines priority. The first matching transition wins. Important Transition helper methods must call OnExitState on the current state and OnEnterState on the new state before returning true. This ensures proper state initialization. Timing Diagram flowchart TD PREV["Frame N-1 contacts"] START["Frame N starts"] SO["SolveOverlaps
Fix penetrations from last frame"] HEI["HandleExternalImpulse
Apply external forces"] INT["OnIntegrateState
Compute velocities + displacement
Uses: last frame's contacts, current input, profile
Produces: HorizontalDisplacement, VerticalDisplacement"] COL["OnResolveStateCollisions × MaxBounce
Uses: displacement from integration
Produces: final position, current frame contacts"] SNAP["SnapToGround
Produces: updated contact if ground found"] CID["ComputeInheritedDisplacement
Produces: platform velocity for next frame"] ET["EvaluateTransitions
Uses: final position, current contacts, input
Produces: state change (if any)"] PREV --> INT START --> SO --> HEI --> INT --> COL --> SNAP --> CID --> ET Next Steps Writing Custom States - How to implement each lifecycle method Physics Configuration - Tuning the physics profile

manual/extending.html.title: Extending the Package | MGM Character Controller
manual/extending.html.summary: Extending the Package The MGM Character Controller is designed to be extended without modifying the package source. This page covers common extension points and patterns. Extension Points 1. Custom States The most common extension. Add new movement behaviors (wall running, swimming, climbing, dashing) by creating new state structs and adding them to your state enum. See Writing Custom States for the full guide. 2. Custom Profiles Your ILocomotionProfile struct can contain any unmanaged data. Add fields for new gameplay parameters: [Serializable] public struct MyProfile : ILocomotionProfile { public MovingProfile Moving; public FallingProfile Falling; public JumpingProfile Jumping; // Custom extension public SwimmingProfile Swimming; public DashProfile Dash; public WallRunProfile WallRun; } Each sub-profile is a plain struct with configuration fields. The data is baked into a blob asset, so add corresponding fields to your LocomotionProfileAsset and BuildBlobAsset method. 3. Custom Context Your context struct can include additional computed properties and helper methods: public struct MyContext : ILocomotionContext<MyProfile, MyInput, MyStateData> { public BaseLocomotionContext BaseContext; public MyStateData StateData; // Standard interface properties... // Custom helpers public bool IsUnderwater => BaseContext.CharacterPosition.Position.y < WaterLevel; public bool WantsToMove => Input.MovementMagnitude > math.EPSILON; public bool CanDash => StateData.DashCooldown <= 0; // Custom ground snap for swimming (no ground snap when submerged) public void ComputeGroundSnappingDistance(in MyProfile profile, out float distance) { if (IsUnderwater) { distance = 0; // No ground snapping while swimming return; } // Normal ground snap calculation... } } 4. Custom StateData Add per-state persistent data for any state-specific tracking: public struct MyStateData : IComponentData { public JumpData JumpData; public float DashCooldown; public float WallRunTimer; public float3 WallNormal; // Remembered from wall run entry } State data persists across frames and state transitions. Clean it up in OnEnterState or OnExitState as appropriate. 5. Custom Input Add input channels for new actions: public struct MyInput : ICharacterLocomotionInput<MyInput> { public float3 Movement; public float JumpTrigger; public float DashTrigger; // New action public float InteractTrigger; // New action public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in MyInput playerInputs) { Movement = playerInputs.Movement; JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger; // Accumulate triggers DashTrigger += playerInputs.DashTrigger; InteractTrigger += playerInputs.InteractTrigger; } } Remember to read the new inputs in your PlayerInputAsset subclass and consume triggers in your state logic. 6. Custom Ground Snapping Override ComputeGroundSnappingDistance on your context to control when and how far the character snaps to the ground: public void ComputeGroundSnappingDistance(in MyProfile profile, out float distance) { // Disable ground snapping while jumping upward if (StateData.CurrentState == MyStates.Jumping && math.dot(BaseContext.VerticalVelocity, -BaseContext.GravityDirection) > 0) { distance = 0; return; } // Default calculation var slopeDistance = math.length(BaseContext.HorizontalVelocity * BaseContext.TimeStep) * math.tan(math.radians(CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxSlope)); distance = math.max(CharacterPhysicsProfile.MaxStepHeight, slopeDistance); } 7. Custom Collision Response Override OnResolveStateCollisions to add custom behavior when the character hits something: public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.Move(); // Custom: bounce off trampolines if (baseContext.IsGroundingContact && HasTrampolineTag(baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.Entity)) { baseContext.VerticalVelocity = -baseContext.VerticalVelocity * 1.5f; } ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } 8. Custom Systems You can add systems that modify locomotion components at any point in the pipeline: [UpdateInGroup(typeof(FixedStepSimulationSystemGroup))] [UpdateBefore(typeof(MyLocomotionSystem))] partial struct GravityZoneSystem : ISystem { public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { foreach (var gravity in SystemAPI.Query<RefRW<LocomotionGravity>>() .WithAll<InGravityZone>()) { gravity.ValueRW.Gravity = new float3(0, -4.9f, 0); // Half gravity } } } Multiple State Machines You can have multiple independent state machine types in the same project. For example: GroundLocomotionStates - Walking, running, jumping, falling VehicleStates - Driving, braking, boosting, airborne SwimmingStates - Surface, submerged, diving Each has its own enum, context, profile, input, state data, and generated systems. They are completely independent. To switch a character between state machines, you would need to add/remove the relevant components (the state machine component, input, profile, state data). This is an advanced pattern that involves structural entity changes. Working with the Source Generator The Roslyn source generator reads attributes on your state enum and generates: State machine component with dispatch logic Input reader system ISystem lifecycle methods (OnCreate, OnUpdate, OnDestroy) on your locomotion system The generator source is at SourceGenerator/SrcGenProj~/. To modify the generated code patterns: Edit StateMachineSourceGenerator.cs Build with dotnet build from the SrcGenProj~ directory The DLL is copied to SourceGenerator/ and picked up by Unity Warning Modifying the source generator affects all state machines in the project. Test thoroughly. Creating Composite Characters For characters that need both ECS locomotion and complex visual hierarchies, use the CompositeEntityAuthoring pattern: The Locomotor entity carries all physics and state machine components A Model entity follows the Locomotor via CharacterFollower The Model's GameObject can have child objects with Animators, particle systems, audio sources The CharacterFollowerAuthoring links the model to the locomotor during baking. At runtime, the CharacterFollowerSystem syncs the transform. Performance Considerations Scaling to Many Characters The locomotion job runs in parallel across chunks. To maximize throughput: Keep all characters with the same archetype (same component set) Use shared colliders (avoid ForceUniqueColliderAuthoring unless needed) Profile shared blob assets (all characters referencing the same profile share memory) Only enable debug visualization on characters you are actively investigating Burst Compilation All state methods should have [BurstCompile]. Common mistakes that prevent Burst compilation: Using string or List<T> types Calling managed virtual methods (DIM lifecycle methods are dispatched by the source generator, not via virtual dispatch) Accessing GameObject or MonoBehaviour APIs Using Debug.Log (use [Conditional("ENABLE_UNITY_COLLECTIONS_CHECKS")] guards) Profiling Use Unity's Profiler to identify bottlenecks: The locomotion job appears as LocomotionJob in the Jobs timeline The ProfilerMarkerExtensions add sub-markers for each execution stage The LocomotionProfilerHUD shows frame-level timing in-game Next Steps Getting Started - Set up your first character Architecture Overview - Understand the full system design Writing Custom States - Implement new movement behaviors

manual/getting-started.html.title: Getting Started | MGM Character Controller
manual/getting-started.html.summary: Getting Started This guide walks you through installing the MGM Character Controller package and getting a playable character running in your scene. Prerequisites Before installing, make sure your project meets these requirements: Unity 6000.3 or later Entities 1.4.2 (with Burst and Jobs) Unity.Physics 1.4.2 Unity.Transforms (included with Entities) The package is designed for Unity's Data-Oriented Technology Stack (DOTS). If you are new to ECS, review Unity's Entities documentation first. You do not need to be an ECS expert, but you should understand the basic concepts of entities, components, systems, and baking. Installation From a local folder Open your Unity project Go to Window > Package Manager Click the + button and select Add package from disk... Navigate to the package.json file inside the MGM Locomotion folder Click Open From a git URL Open Window > Package Manager Click + > Add package from git URL... Enter the repository URL pointing to the Assets/MGM Locomotion folder Project Setup After installation, your project needs a sub-scene with physics enabled. The package relies on Unity.Physics for all collision detection and does not use the legacy physics system. Create or open a scene Add a Sub Scene (right-click in Hierarchy > New Sub Scene) Ensure the sub-scene has a Physics Step component (automatically present in most setups) Add static colliders to your environment (floor, walls, etc.) Creating Your First Character The fastest way to get started is to use the built-in scaffolding tool: Step 1: Generate the State Machine In the Project window, right-click on the folder where you want to create your state machine Select Assets > Create > WAYN Games > Locomotion > StateMachine Name the folder (e.g., MyCharacter) Press Enter The tool generates the entire folder structure with all required scripts: MyCharacter/ ├── Runtime/ │ ├── MyCharacter.runtime.asmdef # Auto-generated assembly definition │ ├── States/ │ │ ├── MyCharacterStates.cs # State enum with source generator attributes │ │ └── MyCharacterState.cs # Initial state implementation │ ├── Data/ │ │ ├── MyCharacterContext.cs # Locomotion context struct │ │ ├── MyCharacterProfile.cs # Profile data struct │ │ ├── MyCharacterInput.cs # Input component struct │ │ ├── MyCharacterStateData.cs # Per-state persistent data │ │ ├── MyCharacterProfileAsset.cs # Profile ScriptableObject │ │ └── MyCharacterPlayerInputAsset.cs # Input ScriptableObject │ └── Systems/ │ ├── IMyCharacterLocomotionSystem.cs # Locomotion system interface │ └── MyCharacterLocomotionSystem.cs # System implementation ├── Authoring/ │ ├── MyCharacter.authoring.asmdef # Auto-generated assembly definition │ ├── MyCharacterCharacterLocomotorAuthoring.cs # Character authoring │ └── MyCharacterPlayerCharacterAuthoring.cs # Player authoring └── Editor/ ├── MyCharacter.editor.asmdef # Auto-generated assembly definition └── MyCharacterStateCreationMenu.cs # Menu for adding new states Step 2: Create the Profile Asset In the Project window, right-click Select the Create menu item for your character profile asset (the menu path is generated for you) Configure movement speed, jump force, gravity multiplier, and other parameters in the Inspector Step 3: Create the Input Asset Right-click in the Project window Select the Create menu item for your player input asset Assign your Input Action References for movement and jump Step 4: Set Up the Character Prefab Create a new GameObject in your sub-scene Add the generated Character Locomotor Authoring component Assign your Profile Asset Configure the Physics Profile (capsule height, radius, mass, max slope, etc.) Step 5: Set Up the Player Prefab The Player is a separate concept from the Character. Players own input and control one or more characters. Create a GameObject outside the sub-scene (it is a regular GameObject, not an entity) Add a Camera, Cinemachine Brain, and Player Input component Add the generated Player Character Authoring component Assign the Character entity, the Input Asset, and the Player GO Prefab Step 6: Enter Play Mode Press Play. Your character should respond to input and interact with the physics environment. If the character falls through the floor, verify that: Your floor has a physics collider The character's collision layers (BelongsTo and CollidesWith) are configured correctly The sub-scene is loaded Importing the Sample The package includes a SinglePlayerSample that demonstrates a complete setup with five states (Idle, Moving, Jumping, Falling, Sliding), a camera rig, moving platforms, and debug visualization. To study the sample: Navigate to Assets/MGM Locomotion/Samples/SinglePlayerSample/ Open the scene in Scenes/ Review the scripts in Scripts/SinglePlayerStateMachine/ for a complete reference implementation Tip The sample is the best resource for understanding how all the pieces connect. Read it alongside this documentation. Next Steps Architecture Overview - Understand the entity types and system design The Execution Loop - Learn what happens each physics tick Writing Custom States - Add new movement behaviors

manual/hybrid-layer.html.title: Hybrid GameObject Layer | MGM Character Controller
manual/hybrid-layer.html.summary: Hybrid GameObject Layer The Hybrid layer bridges the gap between ECS entities and GameObjects. It enables characters driven by Burst-compiled ECS logic to have animated 3D models, particle effects, audio sources, and cameras, all of which require MonoBehaviour and GameObject APIs. Why a Hybrid Layer? Burst-compiled jobs cannot touch GameObjects, Cameras, Animators, or any managed Unity API. But games need these for rendering, animation, audio, and UI. The hybrid layer solves this by: Running all locomotion logic in pure ECS (fast, parallel, Burst-compatible) Syncing entity transforms to GameObjects each frame (main thread, after jobs complete) Letting MonoBehaviours on those GameObjects react to position/rotation changes This means you get the performance of ECS for simulation and the convenience of GameObjects for presentation. Assembly The hybrid layer lives in the Wayn.Games.Locomotion.Hybrid assembly, which references the Runtime assembly. Your game code can reference Hybrid if it needs to interact with the bridge systems. Core Types EntityGameObject (MonoBehaviour) Attached to a GameObject that is linked to an ECS entity. It stores the Entity reference and registers/unregisters the GameObject's Transform with the singleton transform sync system. When the GameObject is destroyed, it automatically unregisters from the sync system to prevent dangling references. EntityGameObjectInstance (MonoBehaviour) A per-instance marker that links a specific GameObject instance to its entity. Used when the same prefab is instantiated multiple times for different entities. GameObjectEntity (Component) An ECS component that stores the UnityObjectRef<GameObject> reference, allowing systems to look up the GameObject from the entity side. EntityGoCleanup (Component) A cleanup component added to entities whose associated GameObjects need to be destroyed. The EntityGoCleanupSystem processes these to ensure GameObjects are properly disposed when their entities are destroyed. Systems EntityGoInitialisationSystem Runs when new entities with GameObjectEntity components appear. It: Instantiates the referenced GameObject prefab Attaches an EntityGameObject MonoBehaviour to link it back to the entity Registers the transform for syncing EntityGoTransformSyncSystem A singleton system that maintains a registry of (Entity, Transform) pairs. Each frame, after all ECS jobs complete, it copies LocalTransform from each registered entity to the corresponding GameObject's Transform. This runs on the main thread because Transform access requires it, but the actual locomotion simulation that produces the positions ran in parallel Burst jobs. EntityGoCleanupSystem Processes entities with the EntityGoCleanup component. When an entity is destroyed, this system ensures the associated GameObject is also destroyed, preventing orphaned GameObjects in the scene. EntityGoPreviewSystem (Editor Only) Provides editor-time preview of entity-linked GameObjects. This allows you to see the visual representation in the Scene view without entering Play mode. Character Follower The CharacterFollower component is an ECS component added to "model" entities. It stores a reference to the Locomotor entity that the model should follow. The CharacterFollowerSystem runs each frame and copies the Locomotor's position and rotation to the model entity. The model entity then has its transform synced to the associated GameObject by EntityGoTransformSyncSystem. This two-hop chain (Locomotor -> Model Entity -> GameObject) ensures: The Locomotor runs in parallel with no GameObject dependencies The Model entity can apply visual offsets or smoothing The GameObject receives the final visual transform Player GameObject Management PlayerGOManagerSystem This system manages the lifecycle of player GameObjects: Spawn: When a new Player entity appears with a PlayerGameObjectPrefab but no PlayerGameObjectInstance, the system instantiates the prefab and stores the instance reference Destroy: When a Player entity is destroyed, the system destroys the associated GameObject The player GameObject typically contains: A Camera component A CinemachineBrain for camera blending A PlayerInput component for input handling A PlayerGoAuthoring with references to the camera and camera target CameraRigFollowControlledCharacterSystem Ensures the player's camera rig follows the controlled character. It reads the ControlledCharacters buffer and updates the camera target position to match the active character's position. DestructionManager A MonoBehaviour that handles cleanup when a hybrid GameObject is destroyed. It ensures all entity references are properly cleaned up and prevents errors from accessing destroyed GameObjects. LocomotionProfilerHUD An optional MonoBehaviour that displays runtime profiling information as an in-game overlay. Useful during development to monitor the performance of the locomotion system. Setting Up a Hybrid Character To create a character with a visual model: 1. Create the Locomotor entity (in sub-scene) Add your Character Locomotor Authoring component as described in Getting Started. 2. Create the Model (in sub-scene) Add a child GameObject under (or near) the Locomotor with your visual mesh/model Add a CharacterFollowerAuthoring component Set the Locomotor field to reference the Locomotor's authoring component The model can have an Animator, AudioSource, particle systems, etc. 3. Create the Player prefab (regular GameObject, NOT in sub-scene) Create a GameObject with Camera, CinemachineBrain, PlayerInput Add a PlayerGoAuthoring component with camera references Save it as a prefab 4. Create the Player entity (in sub-scene) Create a GameObject in the sub-scene Add your Player Character Authoring component Assign the character entity, input asset, and player GO prefab At runtime: The Player entity is baked, triggering PlayerGOManagerSystem to instantiate the player prefab The input asset is initialized with the player's camera and input references The model entity follows the Locomotor via CharacterFollowerSystem The model's GameObject transform is synced via EntityGoTransformSyncSystem Next Steps Architecture Overview - How the entity types relate Input System - How player input flows through the system

manual/input-system.html.title: Input System | MGM Character Controller
manual/input-system.html.summary:
Input System The MGM Character Controller separates input reading from input consumption. This design supports multiple input sources (keyboard, gamepad, AI, network), handles frame rate differences between Update and FixedUpdate, and works cleanly with Unity's new Input System. Architecture Input flows through three layers: flowchart TD A["PlayerInputAsset
Reads hardware input"] B["ICharacterLocomotionInput
Accumulates input per frame"] C["State logic
Reads TInput on context"] A -- "ReadInputsFromAsset(out TInput)" --> B B -- "(generated InputReaderSystem)" --> C A -.- A1["MonoBehaviour layer (Update rate)
e.g., keyboard, gamepad, touch"] B -.- B1["ECS component (Update rate)
ApplyInputsToCharacter()"] C -.- C1["Burst job (FixedUpdate rate)
OnIntegrateState, EvaluateTransitions"] Why Three Layers? Frame rate mismatch: Unity's Update loop runs at variable rate (e.g., 60-144 fps), but FixedUpdate runs at a fixed rate (e.g., 50 fps). If a player presses Jump during a frame where no FixedUpdate runs, that press must not be lost. The accumulation layer ensures trigger inputs survive until the next fixed step. Burst compatibility: PlayerInputAsset is a ScriptableObject that touches MonoBehaviour APIs (Camera, PlayerInput, Input System). These cannot run inside Burst jobs. The separation allows the managed-code layer to read input and the Burst-compiled layer to consume it. Multiple input sources: By abstracting input behind ICharacterLocomotionInput, you can swap the input source without changing any state logic. A keyboard player, a gamepad player, and an AI agent all write to the same TInput component. PlayerInputAsset PlayerInputAsset is an abstract ScriptableObject that you subclass to define how hardware input maps to your input struct. [CreateAssetMenu(menuName = "My Game/Player Input Asset")] public class MyPlayerInputAsset : PlayerInputAsset { public InputActionReference MovementAction; public InputActionReference JumpAction; Camera _camera; PlayerInput _playerInput; public override void ReadInputsFromAsset(out MyInput inputs) { // Read raw input var moveRaw = _playerInput.actions[MovementAction.name].ReadValue(); // Convert to world-space relative to camera Transform cam = _camera.transform; float3 worldMove = cam.forward * moveRaw.y + cam.right * moveRaw.x; worldMove -= math.projectsafe(worldMove, math.up()); // Remove vertical component inputs.Movement = math.normalizesafe(worldMove); // Read trigger inputs.JumpTrigger = _playerInput.actions[JumpAction.name].WasPerformedThisFrame() ? 1f : 0f; } public override void InitInputAsset(PlayerGameObjectInstance instance) { var playerGo = instance.Instance.Value; _camera = playerGo.GetComponent().Camera; _playerInput = playerGo.GetComponent(); _playerInput.ActivateInput(); } } Key points ReadInputsFromAsset is called every Update frame (not FixedUpdate). Return a fresh TInput each call. InitInputAsset is called once when the player is instantiated. Use it to cache references to the camera, PlayerInput, and any other managed objects. The asset is instantiated per player at runtime (ScriptableObject.Instantiate), so each player gets their own cached references. Multiple players on the same machine each have their own copy. Camera-Relative Input Most 3D games convert 2D stick/keyboard input to world-space movement relative to the camera. The pattern is: Read the 2D input vector Multiply by the camera's forward and right vectors Project onto the horizontal plane (remove the gravity-axis component) Normalize This ensures "push up on the stick" always means "move away from the camera," regardless of camera angle. ICharacterLocomotionInput Your input struct must implement this interface: public struct MyInput : ICharacterLocomotionInput { public float3 Movement; public float JumpTrigger; public void ApplyInputsToCharacter(in MyInput playerInputs) { // Continuous inputs: overwrite with latest value Movement = playerInputs.Movement; // Trigger inputs: ACCUMULATE, don't overwrite JumpTrigger += playerInputs.JumpTrigger; } } Input Accumulation The critical distinction is between continuous and trigger inputs: Type Example Behavior Why Continuous Movement direction, aim angle Overwrite each frame Only the latest value matters Trigger Jump press, fire button Accumulate across frames A press during a non-FixedUpdate frame must not be lost If you overwrite JumpTrigger instead of accumulating it, and the player presses Jump during a frame where no FixedUpdate runs, the press is silently discarded. Consuming Triggers In your state's OnIntegrateState, after reading the trigger value, reset it to zero: internal static void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { if (ctx.Input.JumpTrigger > 0) { // Apply jump Jump(ref ctx); } // Consume the trigger MyInput input = ctx.Input; input.JumpTrigger = 0; ctx.Input = input; } Generated Input Reader System The source generator creates an input reader system that: Runs each Update frame (not FixedUpdate) For each player, calls PlayerInputAsset.ReadInputsFromAsset to get raw input For each character controlled by that player, calls ICharacterLocomotionInput.ApplyInputsToCharacter to write to the ECS component You never need to write or modify this system. It is produced automatically from your [LocomotionStateMachine] annotation. AI and Network Input Because input is just an ECS component, you can drive characters from any source: AI Input Create a system that writes directly to the TInput component without going through PlayerInputAsset: [BurstCompile] partial struct AIInputSystem : ISystem { [BurstCompile] public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { foreach (var (input, transform) in SystemAPI.Query, RefRO>() .WithAll()) { // AI decision-making logic input.ValueRW.Movement = ComputeAIMovement(transform.ValueRO); input.ValueRW.JumpTrigger = ShouldJump() ? 1f : 0f; } } } Network Input For networked games, the client sends input to the server, which writes it to the TInput component. The locomotion job runs identically on both client and server, producing deterministic results (assuming the same input and physics state). Cinemachine Integration The sample's LocomotionPlayerInputAsset demonstrates Cinemachine camera setup: Find all CinemachineVirtualCameraBase components on the player prefab Set their Follow and LookAt targets to the character's camera target transform Configure the CinemachineBrain to follow the player's virtual cameras Assign CinemachineInputAxisController player indices for per-player camera control This ensures each player in a split-screen setup has their own camera pipeline. Next Steps Hybrid GameObject Layer - How player GameObjects are managed Writing Custom States - Consuming input in state logic

manual/moving-platforms.html.title: Moving Platforms | MGM Character Controller
manual/moving-platforms.html.summary: Moving Platforms The MGM Character Controller has built-in support for moving and rotating platforms. Characters standing on platforms automatically inherit their velocity and rotation without any special logic in your state code. How It Works Moving platforms are kinematic physics bodies tagged with the Platform component. The locomotion system detects when a character is grounded on a platform and automatically computes the velocity and rotation the character should inherit. The process happens in two steps across two frames: Frame N: Contact Detection During the SnapToGround step, the system detects that the character is standing on a Platform entity. It records the platform's body index and entity reference in the LocomotionContact component. Frame N: ComputeInheritedDisplacement At the end of the locomotion job, the ComputeInheritedDisplacement step reads the platform's current velocity from the physics world and decomposes it into: PlatformHorizontalVelocity - The platform's velocity projected onto the horizontal plane PlatformVerticalVelocity - The platform's velocity along the gravity axis InheritedRotation - The rotational change the character should inherit Frame N+1: Application In the next frame, the character's position is adjusted by the platform's displacement before state logic runs. The inherited rotation is available to your OnIntegrateState for use in rotation logic (e.g., the Idle state's platform spin behavior). Important You should never add platform velocity manually in OnIntegrateState. The system handles it automatically. Adding it yourself would result in double-application, making the character move twice as fast on platforms. Platform Components Platform (Tag) A zero-size tag component marking an entity as a moving platform. Applied during baking by PlatformAuthoring. PlatformMover Stores linear velocity data for the platform. Updated by the PlatformMovementSystem. Rotor Stores angular velocity for rotating platforms. The system applies the rotation each fixed update. Track Constrains platform motion along a predefined path. The platform oscillates between waypoints. Setting Up a Moving Platform 1. Create the platform GameObject (in sub-scene) Create a GameObject with your platform mesh Add a physics collider (Box, Mesh, etc.) Set the collider as Kinematic (not Dynamic, not Static) 2. Add platform authoring Add a PlatformAuthoring component. This tags the entity with the Platform component during baking. 3. Add movement behavior Add a MovingPlatformAuthoring component and configure: Linear Velocity: Direction and speed of movement Angular Velocity: Rotation speed around each axis Track (optional): Define waypoints for the platform to follow 4. Collision layers Ensure the platform's collision layer is included in your character's CollidesWith mask. By default, both use all layers, so this works out of the box. PlatformMovementSystem The PlatformMovementSystem runs in the FixedStepSimulationSystemGroup and updates platform positions and rotations based on their PlatformMover and Rotor components. It writes to the PhysicsVelocity component, which Unity.Physics uses to move the kinematic body. Character Behavior on Platforms Standing Still When idle on a platform, the character moves with the platform. If the platform rotates, the character's rotation is updated by the InheritedRotation (see the sample's IdleState.OnIntegrateState for the spin/roll/slide interpolation logic). Moving on a Platform When walking on a moving platform, the character's own velocity is additive with the platform's velocity. The character moves relative to the platform surface. Jumping off a Platform When the character jumps, the JumpingState.OnEnterState adds PlatformHorizontalVelocity to the character's horizontal velocity: ctx.BaseContext.HorizontalVelocity += ctx.BaseContext.PlatformHorizontalVelocity; This preserves the platform's momentum, so jumping off a fast-moving platform launches the character in the direction the platform was moving. Without this, the character would appear to "snap back" to a standstill mid-air. Platform Push (Overlap Resolution) If a platform moves into the character (e.g., a rising elevator ceiling), the SolveOverlaps step pushes the character out. If the character is trapped between two surfaces (e.g., elevator ceiling and floor), the overlap state transitions through Push -> Pinch -> Crush. Rotating Platform Interaction The SpinOrRollAlongWall parameter on CharacterPhysicsProfile controls how the character responds to being on a rotating platform while touching a wall: SpinOrRollAlongWall = -1: Character spins with the platform, slides along wall SpinOrRollAlongWall = 0: Character slides along wall without rotating SpinOrRollAlongWall = 1: Character "rolls" on the wall surface This is interpolated based on the contact geometry and the relationship between the gravity direction, contact normal, and platform velocity. Custom Platform Behaviors You can create custom platform movement by writing your own system that modifies the PhysicsVelocity component on platform entities. As long as the entity has the Platform tag, the locomotion system will automatically handle character inheritance. For example, a platform that follows a spline: [BurstCompile] partial struct SplinePlatformSystem : ISystem { [BurstCompile] public void OnUpdate(ref SystemState state) { float dt = SystemAPI.Time.fixedDeltaTime; foreach (var (velocity, spline, transform) in SystemAPI.Query<RefRW<PhysicsVelocity>, RefRW<SplineFollower>, RefRO<LocalTransform>>() .WithAll<Platform>()) { float3 targetPos = EvaluateSpline(spline.ValueRO, SystemAPI.Time.ElapsedTime); velocity.ValueRW.Linear = (targetPos - transform.ValueRO.Position) / dt; } } } Gravity Wells The sample includes a GravityWellAuthoring component that modifies the LocomotionGravity component on nearby characters. This is not directly related to platforms but demonstrates how you can modify per-character physics at runtime. Next Steps The Execution Loop - Understand the platform-related steps Physics Configuration - SpinOrRollAlongWall and other platform-relevant settings

manual/physics-configuration.html.title: Physics Configuration | MGM Character Controller
manual/physics-configuration.html.summary: Physics Configuration The CharacterPhysicsProfile defines the physical properties of the character's collision capsule and how it interacts with the world. This page explains each parameter, its effect, and how to tune it. CharacterPhysicsProfile Fields Capsule Geometry Field Type Description CapsuleHeight float Total height of the collision capsule from bottom to top, in world units. The capsule's bottom sphere sits at y = CapsuleRadius and the top sphere at y = CapsuleHeight - CapsuleRadius. CapsuleRadius float Radius of both hemisphere caps. Also determines the minimum width of the character. Default: 0.5. The capsule geometry directly affects: Which gaps the character can fit through How close the character can get to walls Step detection (contact height relative to capsule bottom) Mass and Forces Field Type Description Mass float Weight of the character. Used for collision response calculations when the character collides with dynamic rigid bodies. Higher mass means the character pushes objects more. MassToDynamic float Multiplier for the passive gravitational force the character exerts on dynamic objects. Controls how much the character's presence "pushes" nearby dynamic bodies. Movement Limits Field Type Description MaxSlope float Maximum slope angle (in degrees) the character can walk up. Surfaces steeper than this are treated as walls. Typical values: 45-60 degrees. MaxStepHeight float Maximum height (in world units) of obstacles the character can automatically step over. Must be less than CapsuleHeight - 2 * CapsuleRadius (the flat section of the capsule). Typical values: 0.3-0.5. MaxBounce int Maximum number of collide-and-slide iterations per frame. Each bounce allows the character to redirect along a surface it hits. Higher values handle more complex geometry (corners, narrow corridors) but cost more. Default: 6. Collision Layers Field Type Description BelongsTo LayerMask The physics layer(s) this character belongs to. Other colliders check this to decide if they can collide with the character. CollidesWith LayerMask The physics layer(s) this character tests against. The character only detects collisions with colliders on these layers. To make characters ignore each other, put them on a layer that does not appear in other characters' CollidesWith mask. To make characters collide with each other, include the character layer in both masks. Wall Interaction Field Type Description SpinOrRollAlongWall float [-1, 1] Controls character behavior on a rotating platform when pressed against a wall. At -1, the character spins with the platform while sliding. At 0, the character slides without rotating. At 1, the character "rolls" along the wall surface. Collision Tolerance Field Type Description CollisionTolerance float Skin thickness for all collision queries. This is the distance at which the system considers two surfaces "in contact." Decrease for tighter collision response; increase if you observe jitter or tunneling. Defaults to CollisionWorld.DefaultCollisionTolerance. Interpolation Field Type Description InterpolationMode enum Interpolated enables Unity.Physics graphical smoothing between fixed updates, producing smoother visual movement. NotInterpolated updates the transform directly each fixed step, which can appear jittery at low fixed rates but is simpler to debug. Default Values The static CharacterPhysicsProfile.Default property provides sensible starting values: CharacterPhysicsProfile.Default = new CharacterPhysicsProfile { MaxBounce = 6, SpinOrRollAlongWall = 0, CapsuleRadius = 0.5f, CapsuleHeight = 2f, BelongsTo = 1, // Layer 0 (Default) CollidesWith = ~0, // All layers CollisionTolerance = CollisionWorld.DefaultCollisionTolerance }; Collision Response The character's capsule collider is created with CollisionResponsePolicy.CollideRaiseCollisionEvents. This means: The collider participates in collision detection Collision events are raised (used by CharacterContactSystem for impact detection) The character is not moved by Unity.Physics solver, the locomotion job handles all movement Shared vs Unique Colliders By default, capsule colliders are shared across entities with identical geometry and filter settings. This saves memory when you have many characters with the same shape. If you need a unique collider per entity (e.g., for runtime shape modification), add a ForceUniqueColliderAuthoring component to the character's authoring GameObject. Tuning Guide Character walks through walls Increase MaxBounce (try 8-10) Check CollidesWith includes the wall's layer Increase CollisionTolerance slightly Character jitters on the ground Enable InterpolationMode.Interpolated Check that CollisionTolerance is not too small Verify the ground collider is static (not rebuilding each frame) Character cannot climb steps Increase MaxStepHeight Ensure the step geometry is clean (not sloped mesh artifacts) Check that MaxSlope is high enough that the step face is classified as a wall Character slides off slopes it should walk on Increase MaxSlope (the threshold between "ground" and "wall") Character clips through fast-moving platforms The ground snapping distance may be too small. Override ComputeGroundSnappingDistance in your context to account for higher platform speeds How the Collider Is Built During baking, the BaseCharacterLocomotorBaker constructs the capsule collider from your profile: Creates CapsuleGeometry from CapsuleHeight and CapsuleRadius Creates CollisionFilter from BelongsTo and CollidesWith Sets CollisionResponsePolicy.CollideRaiseCollisionEvents Hashes the geometry + filter to check if a matching blob already exists If not, creates a new CapsuleCollider blob asset Attaches it as a PhysicsCollider component LocomotionGravity Gravity is stored per-entity in the LocomotionGravity component, initialized to PhysicsStep.Default.Gravity during baking. The BaseLocomotionContext decomposes this into: GravityDirection - Normalized direction vector (usually (0, -1, 0)) GravityStrength - Magnitude (usually 9.81) Your state logic reads these to apply gravity acceleration. You can modify the LocomotionGravity component at runtime to change gravity per-character (e.g., for gravity wells, low-gravity zones, or space sections). Next Steps Writing Custom States - Using physics data in state logic Moving Platforms - Platform velocity inheritance

manual/writing-states.html.title: Writing Custom States | MGM Character Controller
manual/writing-states.html.summary: Writing Custom States States are the building blocks of character behavior. Each state defines how the character moves, reacts to physics, and transitions to other states. This guide covers the state interface, lifecycle methods, and common patterns. The State Interface Every state is an unmanaged struct that implements ILocomotionState: [BurstCompile] internal struct MyState : ILocomotionState<MyStates, MyContext, MyProfile, MyInput, MyStateData> { [BurstCompile] public void OnEnterState(ref MyContext ctx) { } [BurstCompile] public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { } [BurstCompile] public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { } [BurstCompile] public void OnExitState(ref MyContext ctx) { } [BurstCompile] public void EvaluateTransitions(ref MyContext ctx, out MyStates newState) { newState = default; } } Key rules: All lifecycle methods are instance methods with default interface implementations (DIM pattern). They are public void, not internal static The source generator dispatches calls to the correct state struct — no virtual dispatch at runtime All methods should have [BurstCompile] for performance The struct itself should have [BurstCompile] No managed types (strings, classes, arrays) — this code runs inside Burst jobs Lifecycle Methods OnEnterState Called once when the state machine transitions into this state. Use it to: Initialize state-specific data (reset timers, counters) Set initial velocities Clear contact information (for airborne states) public void OnEnterState(ref MyContext ctx) { // Entering a grounded state: cancel all vertical momentum ctx.BaseContext.VerticalVelocity = float3.zero; ctx.BaseContext.VerticalDisplacement = float3.zero; } OnIntegrateState Called once per frame during the integration phase. This is where you compute the character's desired displacement based on input, gravity, and profile parameters. Your job here is to populate HorizontalDisplacement, VerticalDisplacement, HorizontalVelocity, and VerticalVelocity on the BaseLocomotionContext. The easiest way is to compute accelerations and call UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements: public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; // Compute gravity float3 gravityAcceleration = baseContext.GravityDirection * baseContext.GravityStrength; // Compute movement acceleration from input float3 desiredVelocity = ctx.Input.MovementDirection * ctx.Profile.MaxSpeed; float accel = ctx.Profile.MaxSpeed / ctx.Profile.TimeToMaxSpeed; MathematicsHelpers.ComputeAccelerationToTargetVelocity( out float3 moveAcceleration, baseContext.HorizontalVelocity, desiredVelocity, baseContext.TimeStep, accel); // Apply both accelerations baseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements( newHorizontalAcceleration: moveAcceleration, newVerticalAcceleration: gravityAcceleration); } Important Do not add platform velocity here. The ComputeInheritedDisplacement step handles it automatically after collision resolution. Adding it manually would cause double-application. The Context Copy Pattern Because BaseLocomotionContext is a value type on the context interface, modifying it requires either: Option A: Use a ref local (when your context exposes the backing field): ref BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ref ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.HorizontalVelocity = float3.zero; // Changes are written through the ref Option B: Copy, modify, write back (when going through the interface): BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.HorizontalVelocity = float3.zero; ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; The sample uses ref access because the context struct exposes BaseContext as a public field in addition to the interface property. OnResolveStateCollisions Called potentially multiple times per frame (up to MaxBounce iterations). Each call represents one iteration of the collide-and-slide algorithm. In most cases, your implementation simply delegates to baseContext.Move(): public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.Move(); ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } Note You must use the copy-modify-writeback pattern here because Move() modifies the context's position, displacement, and contact data internally. For advanced use cases, you can add custom collision response logic. For example, applying an impulse to dynamic bodies the character hits: public void OnResolveStateCollisions(ref MyContext ctx) { BaseLocomotionContext baseContext = ctx.BaseContext; baseContext.Move(); // Push dynamic bodies away on contact if (baseContext.IsInContact && baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.BodyType == BodyType.Dynamic) { float3 pushVelocity = baseContext.HorizontalVelocity * 0.5f; baseContext.ApplyImpulse( ref baseContext, baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.BodyIndex, baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.Entity, /* impactorBodyIndex */ ..., baseContext.CharacterEntity, pushVelocity); } ctx.BaseContext = baseContext; } OnExitState Called once when the state machine transitions out of this state. Use it to clean up state-specific data: public void OnExitState(ref MyContext ctx) { // Reset jump counter when leaving the jumping state ctx.StateData.JumpData.JumpsCount = 0; } EvaluateTransitions Called once at the end of the frame, after all physics is resolved. This method decides whether to change states. public void EvaluateTransitions(ref MyContext ctx, out MyStates newState) { if (IdleToJumping(ref ctx, out newState)) return; if (IdleToMoving(ref ctx, out newState)) return; if (IdleToFalling(ref ctx, out newState)) return; if (IdleToSliding(ref ctx, out newState)) return; } The out newState parameter must always be set. If no transition fires, it defaults to the current state (set newState = default or newState = MyStates.CurrentState). Transition Helper Pattern Each potential transition is implemented as a private static method following the {Source}To{Destination} naming convention: [BurstCompile] private static bool IdleToJumping(ref MyContext ctx, out MyStates newState) { newState = MyStates.Idle; // Default: no transition // Check conditions if (!ctx.BaseContext.IsGroundingContact) return false; if (ctx.Input.JumpTrigger <= 0) return false; // Conditions met: execute transition OnExitState(ref ctx); newState = MyStates.Jumping; JumpingState.OnEnterState(ref ctx); return true; } Critical rules: Set newState to the current state at the top (so it has a value if the transition does not fire) Return false if any condition is not met Call OnExitState on the current state before transitioning Set newState to the target state Call OnEnterState on the target state Return true The order of transition checks in EvaluateTransitions determines priority. Put the most important transitions first (e.g., jump before move, so pressing jump while running works). Consuming Trigger Input Trigger inputs like jump presses must be consumed after use. Otherwise, the trigger fires every frame: static void ConsumeJumpTrigger(ref MyContext ctx) { MyInput value = ctx.Input; value.JumpTrigger = 0; ctx.Input = value; } Call this in OnIntegrateState after using the trigger value. The copy-modify-writeback pattern is required because Input is a value type on the context. Reusing Logic Across States States can share logic via internal static helper methods. For example, the JumpingState in the sample reuses FallingState.ComputeGravityAcceleration for gravity and MovingState.ComputeHorizontalMovementAcceleration for mid-air movement: public void OnIntegrateState(ref MyContext ctx) { // Reuse falling gravity computation FallingState.ComputeGravityAcceleration(ref ctx, terminalVelocity, out float3 gravityAccel); // Reuse moving horizontal acceleration MovingState.ComputeHorizontalMovementAcceleration( ref ctx.BaseContext, ctx.Profile.Moving, ctx.Input.MovementDirection, ctx.Input.MovementMagnitude, out float3 moveAccel); ctx.BaseContext.UpdateVelocitiesAndComputeDisplacements(moveAccel, gravityAccel); } This avoids duplicating physics logic while keeping each state self-contained. Common State Patterns Grounded State (Idle / Moving) OnEnterState: Zero out vertical velocity OnIntegrateState: Compute horizontal acceleration only EvaluateTransitions: Check for jump input, loss of ground contact, slope above max Airborne State (Falling / Jumping) OnEnterState: Clear contact (LocomotionContact.None), optionally inherit platform velocity OnIntegrateState: Apply gravity + optional air control EvaluateTransitions: Check for ground contact, wall contact Sliding State OnIntegrateState: Apply gravity (character slides down slope) EvaluateTransitions: Check for loss of contact (to falling), ground contact below max slope (to moving/idle) Custom State Ideas Wall Running: Enter when jumping into a wall at speed; apply gravity slowly; allow wall jump Dashing: Enter on trigger; apply burst horizontal velocity; exit after timer Swimming: Enter when submerged; replace gravity with buoyancy; allow 3D movement Climbing: Enter at ladder; disable gravity; move along ladder axis Debugging States If your state isn't behaving as expected: Check transition priority order - an earlier transition might be firing first Log baseContext.LocomotionContact.ValueRO.ContactType to verify contact detection Verify that OnExitState and OnEnterState are called during transitions Ensure trigger inputs are accumulated in ApplyInputsToCharacter and consumed in OnIntegrateState Use the debug visualization to see contact points, movement paths, and impulses Next Steps The Execution Loop - When exactly each method is called Physics Configuration - Tuning capsule and collision parameters
